skill.name: secrets-gitleaks
skill.description: Guides teams through hardcoded secret detection with Gitleaks, including repository scans, pre-commit protection, CI templates, custom rules, baselines, false positive handling, and remediation planning.
skill.summary: Use Gitleaks to detect, prevent, and remediate hardcoded secrets in repositories.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly defensive Gitleaks rules, CI templates, and remediation examples rather than malicious code. No prompt injection or data exfiltration intent was found. Publication is acceptable with a warning because the CI examples execute external commands, read scan reports, upload artifacts, and include some fail-open examples.

medium_findings.title: Executable CI Templates Process Repository Contents
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for external command and filesystem access, but legitimate for a Gitleaks skill. The GitHub and GitLab templates run Gitleaks in CI, read generated reports, and upload artifacts. This can expose sensitive findings if artifact access is too broad. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: the commands and file reads are explicit, but their purpose is defensive scanning rather than covert exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Fail-Open Secret Scanning Examples
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE as a workflow risk. Several examples suppress scanner exit codes with exit-code zero or shell true. Some sections add later checks, but users who copy only the command may create secret gates that pass after findings. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: the fail-open commands are visible, while final impact depends on how users copy and deploy the templates.

low_findings.title: Credential Pattern Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for embedded secret theft. The API key, private key, token, and password strings are detection rules or placeholder patterns. They describe what Gitleaks should find and do not contain real credentials. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: the surrounding headings and fields identify these as rule definitions and examples, not operational secrets.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Expected References
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious network behavior. The URLs point to official Gitleaks, OWASP, CWE, pre-commit, GitHub, or CI API resources used by documentation and templates. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: the URLs are visible references or platform endpoints, with no hidden outbound data channel found.

low_findings.title: Malware Keyword Findings Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static malware and C2 keyword hits appear in compliance and remediation reference material about security controls, not in instructions to deploy malware. No prompt injection language was found. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: the reviewed files consistently discuss defensive secret detection and incident response, though broad markdown keyword matches require human context.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Uses External Scanner Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: docker and gitleaks commands run against repository contents. This is expected for secret scanning but should be reviewed before copying into production CI. Confidence: 0.83. Confidence reasoning: command execution is explicit and scoped to Gitleaks scanning.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Reads and Publishes Secret Scan Reports
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: generated Gitleaks reports are parsed, uploaded, or referenced in comments. Reports can contain sensitive metadata and must be access controlled. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: report reads and artifact paths are explicit in the CI templates.

content.user_title: Scan Repositories for Secrets with Gitleaks
content.value_statement: Hardcoded credentials can enter repositories through commits, examples, and configuration files. This skill helps teams add Gitleaks scanning, CI gates, baselines, and remediation workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains repository, directory, staged-change, baseline, and SARIF Gitleaks scan workflows.
Provides GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit configuration templates.
Includes strict, balanced, and custom Gitleaks TOML configuration examples.
Maps common secret findings to CWE, OWASP, PCI-DSS, SOC2, and GDPR concerns.
Guides false positive triage, allowlist design, credential rotation, and git history cleanup.
limitations: Requires Gitleaks and related CI or pre-commit tools to be installed by the user.
Does not rotate credentials or validate whether a detected secret is still active.
Scan reports may contain sensitive findings and need separate access controls.
The file tree does not include the helper scripts described in SKILL.md.

use_cases.title: Audit an Existing Repository
use_cases.description: Run a full-history Gitleaks scan, review findings, classify false positives, and create a baseline for future checks.

use_cases.title: Block Secrets Before Commit
use_cases.description: Install pre-commit scanning so developers catch keys, tokens, and private keys before they reach shared branches.

use_cases.title: Add CI Secret Detection
use_cases.description: Adapt GitHub Actions or GitLab CI templates to publish reports and fail builds when new secrets are detected.

prompt_templates.title: Run My First Secret Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run a Gitleaks scan on this repository and explain the safest report options for a first audit.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Pre-Commit Protection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pre-commit setup plan for Gitleaks that blocks staged secrets and keeps findings redacted.

prompt_templates.title: Tune False Positives
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Gitleaks findings and propose allowlist rules that avoid hiding real credentials.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI Baseline Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions or GitLab CI workflow that uses a baseline, fails only on new secrets, and protects reports.

output_examples.input: I need to scan a legacy repository without blocking every old finding.
output: Recommended approach: run a full scan, store the current findings as a protected baseline, and fail future builds only on new findings.
Treat the baseline as sensitive because it can reveal filenames, rule names, and secret locations.

output_examples.input: A Gitleaks report found a token in git history.
output: Immediate action: rotate the token first, then remove it from current code and clean history if required.
After cleanup, re-scan the repository and document the exposure window, affected systems, and prevention control.

output_examples.input: My scan has many test fixture findings.
output_examples.output: Review a sample of findings, confirm they are placeholders, then add narrow path or stopword allowlists with comments and owners.

best_practices: Always use redacted output for logs, pull request comments, and shared reports.
Rotate exposed credentials before rewriting history or closing the finding.
Keep allowlists narrow, documented, and reviewed by a security owner.
anti_patterns: Do not upload unredacted Gitleaks reports to broadly visible artifacts.
Do not use broad allowlists that exclude all documentation, tests, or configuration files without review.
Do not rely on secret removal alone when a credential may already be compromised.

faq.question: Does this skill run Gitleaks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. The user must run Gitleaks or install the CI and pre-commit integrations.

faq.question: Can it remove exposed secrets from git history?
faq.answer: It explains remediation options, but users must run history cleanup tools and coordinate force pushes themselves.

faq.question: Are scan reports safe to share?
faq.answer: No. Reports can contain sensitive locations and secret metadata. Share only redacted reports with restricted access.

faq.question: Can false positives be ignored?
faq.answer: Only after review. Add narrow allowlists with clear reasons so real credentials are not hidden.

faq.question: Does this replace a secrets manager?
faq.answer: No. It helps find hardcoded secrets. Teams still need a secure secrets manager and rotation process.

faq.question: Is it useful for compliance work?
faq.answer: Yes. The references map hardcoded credential controls to CWE, OWASP, PCI-DSS, SOC2, and GDPR topics.
